book_claims.claim: Rich in lycopene and vitamin C providing antioxidant protection
book_claims.mechanism: Lycopene is a potent carotenoid antioxidant; vitamin C neutralises free radicals

book_claims.claim: Contain lycopene important in prevention of prostate, digestive tract and cervical cancers
book_claims.mechanism: Lycopene reduces IGF-I bioavailability, which may mediate anti-cancer effect. RCT showed reduction in prostate cancer growth with lycopene supplementation before surgery.

supplementary_research.finding: Prospective study of 47,365 men: higher tomato sauce consumption associated with 35% reduced risk of prostate cancer, with lycopene identified as the likely active compound. Two or more servings per week showed the strongest effect.

Review of tomato and lycopene research: processed tomato products (sauce, paste) provide more bioavailable lycopene than raw tomatoes. Strong evidence for cardiovascular and prostate cancer risk reduction.

RCT of lycopene supplementation (30mg/day) in prostate cancer patients before surgery reduced tumor size, PSA levels, and markers of cell proliferation. Directly supports the book's cancer prevention claims.
Lycopene reliably reduces LDL oxidation and blood pressure. Prostate cancer risk reduction is consistently observed in epidemiological studies. Cooked tomatoes with fat maximize absorption.

culinary_pairings.source: Fat enhances lycopene absorption 2-3x; foundational combination in Italian cuisine
Synergistic anti-cancer effect: S-allylcysteine + lycopene inhibit stomach cancer at lower doses than either alone
Insalata caprese; basil's eugenol complements tomato's acidity and adds anti-inflammatory properties
Base of virtually every tomato sauce worldwide; complementary flavonoids
One of the highest-antioxidant herbs; traditional pairing in pizza sauce and Greek salads
Fat from cheese enhances lycopene bioavailability; caprese is a classic combination
Capsaicin and lycopene are both fat-soluble and complementary antioxidants; salsa fresca, arrabbiata

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Cooking and processing dramatically increase bioavailability by breaking cell walls. Fat co-ingestion essential. Tomato paste has 10x the lycopene of fresh tomatoes by weight.
Destroyed by prolonged cooking. Raw tomatoes and brief-cook sauces retain the most. Works synergistically with lycopene as antioxidant.
Tomato paste and sun-dried tomatoes are concentrated sources (~1000 mg/100g). Important for blood pressure regulation.
Anti-inflammatory flavonoid also found in citrus. May contribute to cardiovascular benefits beyond lycopene alone.

synergies.description: Fat from EVOO enhances absorption of fat-soluble lycopene from tomatoes
Traditional pairing providing complementary phytochemical profiles
